question: Describe the design pressure and structure of the CNV.

nuscale_ch05_0024.title: Pressurizer
nuscale_ch05_0024.summary: The pressurizer in the NuScale reactor is integrated into the upper region of the reactor vessel and functions to regulate reactor coolant system (RCS) pressure by maintaining a saturated steam-water interface. It uses heaters and spray flow to control pressure and is designed to handle operating transients without triggering reactor trips or safety system actuations, providing pressure and level information to monitoring and control systems.

nuscale_ch05_0014.title: Reactor Vessel
nuscale_ch05_0014.summary: The Reactor Vessel section describes the NuScale Power Module's reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which houses the reactor core, steam generators, and pressurizer within a single vessel surrounded by a containment vessel. The RPV serves as a key pressure-retaining component and barrier to fission product release, supporting various reactor systems and constructed according to ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code standards to ensure integrity throughout the plant's design life. Material specifications for the RPV and its components are selected to maintain safety and compliance with regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0004.title: System Description
nuscale_ch05_0004.summary: The Reactor Coolant System (RCS) in the NuScale design is located within the containment vessel and includes key components such as the reactor pressure vessel, pressurizer, and associated piping and controls. It removes heat from the reactor core to the steam generators using natural circulation, and features systems for removing noncondensible gases and maintaining pressure boundary integrity to prevent radionuclide release. The RCS interfaces with several other systems, including emergency core cooling and chemical control, to ensure safe and efficient operation.

nuscale_ch05_0011.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Inservice Inspection and Testing
nuscale_ch05_0011.summary: This section describes the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in key reactor coolant system components, emphasizing the use of Alloy 718 for its compatibility and stress corrosion cracking resistance. It also outlines the requirements for preservice and inservice inspection and testing of reactor coolant pressure boundary components, specifying adherence to the latest ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) editions as mandated by regulatory requirements.

nuscale_ch05_0018.title: References
nuscale_ch05_0018.summary: This section outlines the requirements and procedures for inspection, shipment, installation, operating conditions, inservice surveillance, and threaded fasteners for the NuScale reactor vessel, referencing relevant ASME and ASTM standards. It specifies that the reactor vessel must be shipped and stored in a controlled, dry, and pressurized environment, with measures to exclude foreign materials and maintain integrity. Key references include the ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code, ASME NQA-1 quality assurance standards, and ASTM practices for material testing.

nuscale_ch05_0009.title: Overpressure Protection
nuscale_ch05_0009.summary: This section outlines NuScale's compliance with overpressure protection requirements by referencing applicable ASME Boiler and Pressure Vessel Code (BPVC) and Operations and Maintenance (OM) code cases, as mandated by various sections of 10 CFR 50.55a and related Regulatory Guides (RGs). It details how code cases for design, fabrication, construction, preservice, and inservice inspections and testing are selected and verified to meet NRC requirements, with a consolidated list provided in Table 5.2-1.

nuscale_ch05_0003.title: Design Basis
nuscale_ch05_0003.summary: The Design Basis section outlines that the NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) uses natural circulation without pumps or external piping, ensuring safe and efficient heat transfer from the reactor core to the steam generator. The RCS is designed to maintain proper coolant flow, water chemistry, and boron concentration to protect fuel integrity and support safe operation under normal and transient conditions.

nuscale_ch05_0019.title: Reactor Coolant System Component and Subsystem Design
nuscale_ch05_0019.summary: This section describes the design of the NuScale Power Module’s Reactor Coolant System (RCS) components and subsystems, including the reactor pressure vessel, steam generators, safety and ECCS valves, and associated piping. It details the integration of these components within the RPV, highlights the boundaries between primary and secondary systems, and references other sections for further design specifics.

nuscale_ch05_0012.title: Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary Leakage Detection
nuscale_ch05_0012.summary: The NuScale reactor coolant system (RCS) does not use traditional components with designed leakage rates, making it impractical to distinguish between identified and unidentified leakage inside containment using current instrumentation. Any leakage into containment, regardless of source, quickly vaporizes and disperses, so all leakage is treated as unidentified until specifically located and quantified by other means; leakage detection relies on RCS inventory balance and comparison to containment evacuation system (CES) flow rates, with operational limits set in technical specifications. The installed leakage detection systems are reliable, redundant, and sensitive enough to support leak-before-break safety analyses.

edges.description: Section B explains that the containment vessel (CNV) surrounds the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which includes the pressurizer described in Section A, thus establishing the physical and functional relationship between the pressurizer's design and the CNV's structure.

Section A describes the pressurizer's structure and its integration with the reactor vessel, while Section B explains how the pressurizer's steam space is vented to the CNV during ECCS operation, connecting the pressurizer's design to the CNV's pressure boundary function.

Section A describes the pressurizer as an integral part of the reactor vessel, while Section B specifies the material and design standards for threaded fasteners used in components including the pressurizer, connecting the structural design of the CNV to its pressure boundary integrity.

Section A describes the pressurizer as an integral part of the reactor vessel, while Section B provides requirements and procedures for the reactor vessel's inspection, shipment, and storage, connecting them through their shared focus on the reactor vessel's structure and integrity.

Section A describes the pressurizer's structural integration and function within the reactor vessel, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that govern the design and integrity of reactor coolant boundary components like the pressurizer, connecting them through compliance requirements.

Section A provides specific structural and functional details about the pressurizer, which is identified in Section B as an integral component of the reactor coolant system (RCS) and the NuScale Power Module (NPM).

Section A provides specific details about the pressurizer's structure and function, which is one of the components generally described in Section B's overview of the reactor pressure vessel and its subsystems.

Section A describes the physical arrangement of the CNV surrounding the RPV, while Section B explains how the RCS interfaces with the CNV during specific operations (e.g., venting to the CNV), connecting the structural design to its functional use.

Section A provides a general description of the reactor vessel and its relationship to the containment vessel (CNV), while Section B specifies the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in the reactor pressure vessel, which are structural components relevant to the vessel's integrity.

Section B references Section A by specifying that the nondestructive examinations performed on the reactor vessel are described in Section 5.3.1, which is part of the broader reactor vessel description in Section A.

Section A describes the physical structure and arrangement of the CNV in relation to the reactor vessel, while Section B outlines the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that govern the design and overpressure protection requirements for such vessels, connecting the vessel's design to its compliance obligations.

Section A describes the physical arrangement of the CNV surrounding the RPV, while Section B provides a general overview of the reactor coolant system (RCS) components, including the RPV but does not detail the CNV, thus Section A specifies structural details that Section B only generally references.

Section A provides a general overview of the CNV's relationship to the RPV within the NPM, while Section B specifies the internal components and subsystems contained within the RPV, both contributing to an understanding of the CNV's design context.

Section A describes the function and pressure-retaining role of the RCS and its components, while Section B specifies the material and code requirements for the threaded fasteners used in those pressure-retaining structures, connecting the structural integrity of the CNV to compliance with ASME standards.

Section A describes the system function and pressure boundary role of the RCS and CNV, while Section B specifies the inspection, shipment, and storage requirements for the reactor vessel, both referencing the structural integrity and regulatory compliance of pressure-retaining components.

Section A describes the system features that maintain the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, while Section B specifies the regulatory codes and standards (ASME BPVC, 10 CFR 50.55a) that the design and construction of these pressure-retaining structures, including the CNV, must comply with.

Section A provides specific operational details about the RCS and its interaction with the CNV, while Section B gives a general design basis overview of the RCS, establishing the context for the details in Section A.

Section A describes the function and operation of the degasification and venting lines that discharge to the CNV, while Section B specifies the structural components and subsystems of the RCS, including those lines, thus providing the physical context for the design features mentioned in Section A.

Section A describes the material and design standards for threaded fasteners in the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), while Section B outlines the inspection, shipment, and handling requirements for the RPV, both referencing ASME BPVC standards to ensure structural integrity.

Section A describes the materials and code compliance for threaded fasteners in the CNV's pressure boundary, while Section B outlines the use of ASME BPVC code cases and regulatory compliance for the design and construction of the same boundary, connecting them through their shared reliance on ASME BPVC requirements for structural integrity.

Section A provides specific material and design details for threaded fasteners used in the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which is a structural component described more generally as part of the reactor coolant system (RCS) in Section B.

Section A describes the materials and standards for threaded fasteners used in the RPV and references Section 3.13 for further design details, while Section B provides an overview of the RPV's components and subsystem design, thus Section A provides specific material and compliance information that supports and specifies the general component design described in Section B.

Section A references the ASME BPVC Section III code for shipment and installation of the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B discusses compliance with the same ASME BPVC Section III code for design, fabrication, and construction, connecting them through the shared regulatory code governing the CNV's structural integrity and pressure requirements.

Section A references the ASME BPVC and NQA-1 standards for the shipment and installation of the reactor pressure vessel (RPV), which is a structural component described in Section B as part of the reactor coolant system (RCS) design basis.

Section A references the ASME BPVC and NQA-1 standards for shipment and handling of the reactor pressure vessel, while Section B describes the structural components and systems contained within the reactor pressure vessel, connecting them through the shared subject of the RPV's design and regulatory compliance.

Section A references the regulatory codes and standards (such as ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a) that govern the design and construction of the CNV, while Section B provides a summary description of the reactor coolant system's structure, which includes the CNV as a component designed in accordance with those codes.

Section A outlines the regulatory codes and standards (such as ASME BPVC and 10 CFR 50.55a) that must be followed for the design, fabrication, and construction of reactor coolant boundary components, while Section B describes the physical components and subsystems of the reactor coolant system, which must be designed in compliance with those codes and standards.

Section B provides detailed component and subsystem design information that specifies the general system description outlined in Section A, particularly regarding the structure and configuration of the reactor coolant system relevant to the CNV.

Section B describes the structural design and pressure communication function of the pressurizer, which is a component of the Reactor Coolant Pressure Boundary discussed in Section A regarding leakage detection.

Section B describes the structural arrangement of the CNV as surrounding the reactor pressure vessel, while Section A discusses leakage detection relevant to the integrity of the reactor coolant pressure boundary, which is housed within the CNV structure described in Section B.

Section A discusses leakage detection challenges related to the reactor coolant pressure boundary (RCPB), while Section B describes the system design features (such as pressure-retaining portions and venting) that maintain the integrity and pressure of the RCS and CNV, thus providing context for the design pressure and structure referenced in the analysis question.
